nativeRange.habitat: Found on steep outer reef slopes and walls, typically at depths of 12–40 m, where light levels are moderate and currents can be substantial.

diet.notes: Planktivore in the wild; generally advised to offer frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched frozen brine shrimp, and high-quality marine pellets multiple times daily. A refugium cultivating copepods and amphipods has been reported to improve long-term health and color.

summary: The Pictilis Anthias is a brilliantly colored reef fish from the outer slopes of the Western Pacific, where it lives in harems above steep drop-offs. Intermediate care is required, primarily due to its need for frequent small feedings and careful acclimation.
careNotes: Pictilis Anthias are protogynous hermaphrodites — all individuals are born female, and if the dominant male is lost the largest female will often transition to male. They have been known to jump from open aquaria and a secure lid is advisable. Best kept as a harem group (one male with 2–4 females); two males in the same tank tend to result in serious aggression. Initial acclimation may be challenging: new arrivals commonly prefer lower lighting and multiple hiding spots. Feeding frequency is higher than many marine fish — three to five small feedings per day is generally advised. A refugium producing live copepods and amphipods has been reported to meaningfully improve long-term condition. Males display notably more vivid coloration and elongated dorsal fin filaments compared to females. In the wild this species forms aggregations above steep outer reef slopes at depths of roughly 12–40 m, suggesting it is adapted to moderate currents and somewhat dimmer light than typical shallow reef species.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been widely reported. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite; large females will transition to male when the dominant male is removed. Breeding in aquaria would require a large, stable system with a well-established harem group.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (13.5 cm TL), temperature range (22–27°C), native range, and taxonomy. FishBase is authoritative for max length and temperature; range data from FishBase used as primary reference.

additional.name: TankFacts
additional.notes: Additional cross-check for temperature (76–80°F = 24.4–26.7°C), salinity (SG 1.023–1.026), pH (7.8–8.4), and breeding behavior. Temperature and salinity averaged across all three sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has rarely been reported for this species. Although sex change occurs naturally — the largest female will transition to male if the dominant male is lost — successfully inducing spawning in captivity is considered difficult and would require a large, stable system with a well-established harem group.
